title: What Is a SETA in South Africa? Complete Guide 2026
intro: A SETA — Sector Education and Training Authority — is a government body that funds skills training in South Africa. There are 21 SETAs, each responsible for a different sector of the economy.
body:
SETAs are funded by a 1% payroll levy that every employer with a large enough payroll pays to SARS. This money goes into a Skills Development Fund. SETAs use this fund to pay companies to train unemployed people through learnerships, apprenticeships, and skills programmes.

How Do SETAs Help Job Seekers?

When a company runs a SETA-funded learnership, they take on unemployed people as learners. You receive on-the-job training AND a nationally recognised qualification — free of charge, with a monthly stipend. You never apply to the SETA directly; you apply to companies running SETA-funded programmes.

Which SETA Covers Which Sector?

Each SETA covers a specific industry. For IT learnerships, look for MICT SETA programmes. For banking, BANKSETA is relevant. For construction, CETA. For retail, W&RSETA. You'll find the SETA name listed on every opportunity in our directory.

Do I Need to Pay Anything?

No. All legitimate SETA-funded learnerships are completely free of charge. If anyone asks you to pay an application fee, registration fee, or any deposit — it is a scam. Report it to the SAPS immediately.

faq.q: How many SETAs are there in South Africa?
faq.a: There are 21 SETAs, each covering a different economic sector.

faq.q: Can I apply directly to a SETA for a learnership?
faq.a: No. You apply to companies or training providers that run SETA-funded programmes, not to the SETA itself.
Yes. All legitimate SETA-funded programmes are completely free. Any programme that charges a fee is a scam.

meta_title: What Is a SETA in South Africa? Complete 2026 Guide
meta_description: Learn what a SETA is, how SETAs fund learnerships, and how to access SETA opportunities in South Africa. Clear, plain-language guide updated for 2026.

title: What Is a Learnership in South Africa? 2026 Explained
intro: A learnership is a structured training programme combining classroom learning with real work experience. You earn a nationally recognised qualification AND a monthly stipend — and it's completely free to apply for.
body:
Learnerships are funded through South Africa's SETA system. When a company runs a learnership, they receive a government grant to cover the cost of training and employing a learner. This is why learnerships exist: government incentivises companies to train people who would otherwise not get work experience.

How Long Does a Learnership Last?

Most learnerships run for 12 months. Some technical learnerships in engineering, manufacturing, and mining can run 18 to 24 months. At the end, you receive a SAQA-registered NQF qualification.

What Is a Learnership Stipend?

A stipend is the monthly allowance paid to learners during training. In 2026, most learnerships pay between R3 500 and R8 000 per month, depending on the sector and NQF level. Stipends are not subject to PAYE tax.

Who Can Apply?

Most learnerships require: South African citizen with a valid ID, Matric certificate, currently unemployed, aged 18–35. Some require specific subjects. Check the eligibility section of each listing carefully.

faq.q: How long does a learnership last?
faq.a: Most learnerships run for 12 months. Technical learnerships may run up to 24 months.

faq.q: Can I do a learnership if I already have a degree?
faq.a: Yes, but you'll likely qualify for higher-level learnerships or graduate internships instead.

faq.q: Is a learnership the same as an apprenticeship?
faq.a: No. An apprenticeship leads specifically to a trade certificate (artisan qualification), while a learnership leads to a broader NQF qualification.

meta_title: What Is a Learnership? South Africa Guide 2026
meta_description: A learnership pays you a monthly stipend while you earn an NQF qualification. Find out how learnerships work, who can apply, and how to get one in South Africa.

title: How to Apply for Learnerships: Step-by-Step Guide 2026
intro: Applying for a learnership doesn't have to be complicated. This step-by-step guide covers everything — from checking your eligibility to submitting your application. Never pay to apply.
body:
Step 1: Check Your Eligibility

Confirm you meet the requirements: South African ID, Matric certificate, unemployed status, and the correct age range. Some learnerships require specific Matric subjects.

Step 2: Gather Your Documents

You will need: certified copy of your ID (within 3 months), certified Matric certificate, CV of maximum 2 pages, motivational letter of 1 page, and proof of residence not older than 3 months.

Step 3: Write a Strong Motivational Letter

Most applicants fail at this step by sending a generic letter. Your letter must name the specific company and programme, explain why you want this particular opportunity, and describe what you bring to it. Tailor every letter — generic ones are rejected.

Step 4: Submit via the Official Website

Apply only through the company's official careers page. Email subject format: Application: [Learnership Name] — [Your Full Name]. Send before the closing date — late applications are never considered.

Step 5: Follow Up Once

One week after applying, send a short follow-up email confirming your application. Once only — multiple follow-ups create a negative impression.

faq.q: How many learnerships can I apply for at once?
faq.a: You can apply for as many as you like, but you can only be enrolled in one at a time.

faq.q: What if I don't have a printer for documents?
faq.a: Internet cafés and print shops will print and certify your documents for a small fee. The post office offers free certification services.

meta_title: How to Apply for a Learnership in South Africa: Step-by-Step 2026
meta_description: Complete step-by-step guide to applying for learnerships in South Africa. Documents needed, motivational letter tips, and how to find official applications.

title: How to Avoid Learnership Scams in South Africa (2026)
intro: Learnership scams are widespread in South Africa. Fake opportunities advertised on social media cost job seekers thousands of rands every year. Here's how to protect yourself.
body:
The most important rule: legitimate learnerships in South Africa are ALWAYS free to apply for. Any programme that asks you to pay anything before you start is a scam.

7 Warning Signs of a Learnership Scam

1. An application fee is requested. No legitimate SETA-funded learnership ever charges a fee to apply.

2. Documents requested before an interview. Real programmes only request documents at the interview stage or after an offer.

3. The stipend is unusually high. A learnership advertising R25 000/month is almost certainly fake. Typical stipends are R3 500 to R8 000.

4. WhatsApp-only contact from an unknown number. Legitimate employers contact applicants via official company email addresses.

5. No official company website. Always verify the company exists at cipc.co.za before engaging.

6. Urgency pressure. Apply in the next 24 hours or lose your spot. Legitimate programmes have published closing dates, not artificial urgency.

7. The email domain is generic. setalearnerships@gmail.com is not an official company email. Real employers use @companyname.co.za addresses.

How to Verify a Legitimate Opportunity

Search the company name on Google and go directly to their official website. Look for the opportunity on their official careers page. If it's not there, treat it as suspicious. Verify company registration at cipc.co.za.

faq.q: Is it safe to apply via Facebook adverts?
faq.a: Be very cautious. Always cross-reference the advert with the company's official website before applying or providing any documents.

faq.q: Are all listings on OpportunitiesZA verified?
faq.a: Yes. Every listing on this site links to the official company or government source. We mark each listing as Verified Source once confirmed.

meta_title: How to Avoid Learnership Scams in South Africa 2026
meta_description: Learn to identify fake learnerships in South Africa. Know the 7 red flags, how to verify opportunities, and what to do if you've been scammed.

title: NQF Levels Explained: What They Mean for Your Career (2026)
intro: The NQF — National Qualifications Framework — classifies all South African qualifications from Level 1 to Level 10. Understanding it helps you know exactly which opportunities you qualify for.
body:
The National Qualifications Framework (NQF) is South Africa's system for classifying all education and training qualifications. Every qualification sits at a specific level. This matters because every opportunity listing states a minimum NQF level or qualification type.

The Key Levels

NQF Level 4 — Matric (Grade 12 / NSC). The most common entry level for learnerships. Most SETA learnership programmes lead to an NQF Level 4 qualification.

NQF Level 5 — Higher Certificate. A one-year post-Matric qualification. Opens more learnership and some internship doors.

NQF Level 6 — Diploma / Advanced Certificate. A three-year TVET or university diploma. Required for many graduate internship programmes.

NQF Level 7 — Bachelor's Degree. A three-year university degree. Required for corporate graduate programmes and professional internships.

TVET and Learnerships

Many SETA learnerships specifically target TVET graduates — particularly N6 graduates who need the 18-month practical component for their National Diploma. A TVET-funded learnership allows you to complete your diploma while being paid a stipend.

faq.q: Does a learnership give me an NQF qualification?
faq.a: Yes. Completing a SETA-registered learnership results in a SAQA-registered qualification at a specific NQF level — most commonly NQF Level 4.

faq.q: Is a TVET diploma equivalent to a university degree?
faq.a: A 3-year TVET diploma sits at NQF Level 6, while a university degree sits at NQF Level 7. They are different qualifications, but a TVET diploma is widely respected and qualifies you for many graduate opportunities.

meta_title: NQF Levels Explained — South Africa 2026 Guide
meta_description: Understand South Africa's NQF qualification levels and what they mean for learnerships, bursaries, and career opportunities. Plain-language guide.

title: NSFAS 2026: How to Apply, Who Qualifies & What It Covers
intro: NSFAS covers tuition, accommodation, and a living allowance for students at public universities and TVET colleges. No repayment required for most students.
body:
Who Qualifies for NSFAS in 2026?

You qualify if: you are a South African citizen, your household income is R350 000 or less per year, and you are registered or planning to register at a public university or TVET college. SASSA grant recipients qualify automatically — no income proof needed.

What Does NSFAS Cover?

For university students: tuition fees, accommodation (in university residences), meal allowance, transport allowance, and personal care allowance. For TVET college students: tuition and a monthly living allowance.

How to Apply

Applications open October–November each year at myNSFAS.org.za — the only legitimate application portal. Never use agents claiming to apply on your behalf. Any agent charging a fee is running a scam. NSFAS applications are free.

NSFAS vs Private Bursary

Apply for both — but double funding is not allowed. If you receive a full corporate bursary covering all costs, you cannot also receive NSFAS. Disclose all funding applications honestly on each form.

faq.q: What happens if my NSFAS application is rejected?
faq.a: You can appeal through the myNSFAS portal within the appeal period. Most rejections are due to incomplete applications. Submit all documents correctly the first time.
No. NSFAS only covers registered public universities and public TVET colleges.

meta_title: NSFAS 2026 Application: Who Qualifies, What It Covers & How to Apply
meta_description: Everything you need to know about NSFAS 2026 — who qualifies, what is covered, how to apply at myNSFAS, and how it compares to private bursaries.

title: How to Write a CV for a Learnership (With Structure)
intro: Your CV is the first filter between you and a learnership. A clean, 2-page CV with the right information gives you a real advantage over hundreds of applications.
body:
CV Structure for Learnership Applicants

Section 1 — Personal Details: Full name, ID number, contact number, email address, physical address, province. Do not include a photo unless specifically requested.

Section 2 — Education: Institution name, year completed, relevant subjects and symbols. List Matric first. If you have a TVET qualification or any other certificate, list it too.

Section 3 — Experience: Any work experience, even informal, part-time, or volunteer. Include company name, your role, dates, and 2–3 bullet points. No experience? Include community work or school projects.

Section 4 — Skills: Basic computer literacy, languages spoken, driver's licence if applicable. Keep it brief and honest.

Section 5 — References: Two references — a teacher, community leader, or family friend (not a parent). Include name, relationship, and phone number.

Non-Negotiable CV Rules

Maximum 2 pages. Font: Arial or Calibri, size 11–12. No spelling errors. Save as PDF — not Word (.doc). File name: YourName-CV.pdf. No photo unless requested.

faq.q: Should I include a photo on my CV?
faq.a: Only if the application specifically asks for one. Adding an unrequested photo wastes space.

faq.q: What if I have no work experience at all?
faq.a: Include volunteer work, helping at a family business, school projects, or community involvement. Something is always better than leaving it blank.

meta_title: How to Write a CV for a Learnership — South Africa 2026
meta_description: Write a winning CV for learnership applications in South Africa. Structure, rules, and tips — free guide for first-time applicants.
